Implementing Cognitive Procurement and its Influence on Supply Chain
During the Era of Digital Transformation in Oil and Gas Industry 4.0
Manoj Kannankutty, ADNOC Distribution; Aditya Manoj Menon, Abu Dhabi International School
Copyright 2021, Society of Petroleum Engineers
This paper was prepared for presentation at the Abu Dhabi International Petroleum Exhibition & Conference to be held in Abu Dhabi, UAE, 15 – 18 November 2021.
The official proceedings were published online on 9 December 2021.
This paper was selected for presentation by an SPE program committee following review of information contained in an abstract submitted by the author(s). Contents
of the paper have not been reviewed by the Society of Petroleum Engineers and are subject to correction by the author(s). The material does not necessarily reflect

Abstract
The digital transformation is key for every oil and gas organization to implement its fourth industrial
revolution (Industry 4.0). Digital transformation in procurement uses artificial intelligence, big data, market
intelligence, etc. to create self-learning systems; for the procurement of goods and services. This is also
known as cognitive procurement or procurement 4.0.
The objective of this paper is to examine the methods of implementing cognitive procurement, the impact
it creates in the procurement life cycle, and in the supply chain.
The paper also provides insight into three governance models of human-machine interaction that
is implemented in stages based on procurement requirement and technology adoption maturity of the
organization.
• Human-in-the-loop model
• Human on-the-loop model
• Human out-of-loop model
The data for this study is obtained by preparing a list of relevant questions from multiple sources. An
online survey questionnaire is sent to the procurement professionals in different organizations in the oil and
gas industry. The questions reveal procurement expert's responses for:
• Potential challenges for the digital transformation in procurement and supply chain.
• Methods of enabling technologies to leverage procurement procedures and processes.
The results and the observations of the study are used to measure the benefits of implementing cognitive
procurement in the oil and gas industry 4.0. The new transformed roles of procurement for industry 4.0 are
also discussed in the conclusion, which includes:
• Daily activities

Natural Language Processing (NLP) (Advani 2021)
Natural Language Processing employs Computational Linguistics (CL) together with machine learning,
statistical, and deep learning models to read, understand, and interpret natural language.
Chat bots use natural language processing to interpret the intent of the user and respond accordingly.
They rely on NLP to accomplish their tasks. NLP in conjunction with other AI subsets, can save significant
time for contract management by reviewing legal contracts and verifying the compliance.
Computer Vision (CV)
Computer vision uses algorithms to interpret various attributes of an image by breaking down the image
and studying different parts of the objects. This helps the machine to analyze, classify and learn from a set
of images, to make a better output decision based on previous observations. A good example for computer
vision is Google's vision API that is used to understand and interpret the attributes of image.
Computer vision could help approve payment as per the actual progress for a construction project based
on the real time progress image obtained from the construction site.
Big Data
Big data is a combination of structured, semi structured and unstructured high-volume data collected form
internal and external sources.
Big data is often characterized by the three V's (Emmanuel et al. 2016):
• The large Volume of data from multiple sources
• The wide Variety of data types frequently stored in big data systems
• The Velocity, at which much of the data is generated, collected and processed.
In a nutshell, big data is a solution for analyzing huge volume of structured, semi-structured and
unstructured data from various sources with variety of data types which are generated, collected and
processed at high velocity.
The big data have major role to play in successful implementation of procurement 4.0.
Internet of Things (IoT)
The Internet of things (IoT) (Xia, F et al. 2012)is a system of interrelated computing devices, mechanical
and digital machines, objects, animals or human beings that are embedded with sensors, software, and other
technologies to obtain unique identifiers for the purpose of connecting and exchanging data with other
devices and systems over the Internet.
Robotic Process Automation (RPA)
Robotic process automation (van der Aalst et al. 2018) is a software technology that helps to build, deploy,
and manage software robots that emulate human actions. As an example, RPA can be used to read paper-
based supplier delivery notes and enter into the system without human interaction.
Blockchain
Blockchain uses decentralized ledger technology for recording transactions by using a chain of blocks. Each
block can hold a transaction and copies of the transaction are available to all participants of the blockchain.
The new blocks are added to the chain by using hash created from the last block. This way the transactions
are irreversible and secured in the block.
Blockchain can be used in procurement (King 2020) to record secure transactions for tracking a product
or transaction from source of acquisition to payment. A new block is added to the chain for each transaction

down the supply chain. Many innovations can be initiated in procurement processes and supply chain
management using blockchain technology.
Cloud Computing
In Cloud Computing (Armbrust, M. et al. 2010), software, analytics, cyber security, and intelligence are
delivered over the Internet to allow quicker innovation, flexible resources, the exchange of data, and cost
savings.
This eliminates the need for companies to invest in IT infrastructure data centers and networks. In Cloud
Computing, the subscriber pays only for cloud services they use, helping to lower their operating costs, run
their infrastructure more efficiently and scale as their business needs change.
Cognitive Procurement
Digital transformation in procurement uses artificial intelligence, Big Data, market intelligence, etc. to
create self-learning systems; for procurement of goods and services. This is also known as cognitive
procurement or procurement 4.0.
Digital transformation in procurement is as important to oil and gas companies as transformation efforts
in manufacturing, marketing and sales. This is simply because any savings in procurement has a huge impact
on the oil and gas company's financial bottom line.
Currently, the procurement department of most oil and gas companies relies on the expertise of
procurement and business staff to identify suppliers for procurement needs. The organization manages
purchasing and contracts mainly using internal data from the organization's Enterprise Resource Planning
(ERP) system.
However, most oil and gas companies do not have mandatory policies to collect Market Intelligence
(MI) and Big Data from external sources to obtain insight of alternative suppliers, alternative supply chain,
cost, value, quality of service delivery, etc. before issuing purchase orders or awarding contracts. Such
information benchmarked against competitors at regional and global levels provides the company with
additional insights for better decision-making before awarding contracts or purchase orders.
The oil and gas companies can use cognitive computing to provide faster and more accurate procurement
analytics. This will also lead to improved operational efficiency and tactical purchasing. Cognitive
computing should also help to identify supplier risks and opportunities. Fig. 5 illustrates cognitive
computing in procurement.

from China. To make production fail-safe, oil and gas companies should have access to many data sources to
have information on alternative products, suppliers, manufacturing countries, market information, probable
supply chain delays that affect the procurement of critical components. This way, the organization can be
prepared with risk mitigation plans to handle the situations without any major loss of production.
AI in procurement
Artificial intelligence can be used to automatically review and approve purchase orders.
Automation of payments
Machine learning can be used for automated payment approvals to reduce the invoice payment cycle.
AI Spend Analysis Software
In purchasing, ML algorithms can be used in software for faster processes such as classifying spend and
matching with suppliers.
New supplier identification
Big Data techniques are enabling new ways to identify, manage and use supplier data in public and
private databases. Market intelligence from multiple sources combined with Big Data and machine learning
algorithms can improve the identification of new suppliers for procurement needs.
AI in Strategic Sourcing
Artificial Intelligence along with Big Data, market intelligence can act as a sourcing advisor. This can help
procurement to get better insight for better negotiations and informed decisions.
AI in contract management
Natural language processing combined with machine learning can check contract documents for legal
compliance. The AL can report potential compliance violations and the expert can verify and confirm them.
This significantly shortens contract review time and reduces contract management costs.
Methods for implementing cognitive sourcing
Human On The Loop (HOTL)
Human on the Loop is a primitive process that most companies still use. This is due to the static nature
of procurement in the organizations. In this process, the procurement specialist only makes predictions
and decisions about which external source is best for procuring goods, services or labor. Therefore, there
is no AI system that reviews the data and helps the procurement specialist make an informed decision.
Instead, specialists must review the data themselves to shortlist procurement providers before making a
final, informed decision. The disadvantage of this type of system is that humans are prone to errors and
inaccuracies when it comes to analyzing the data. There is also the possibility of vested interests, which
could lead to a biased decision that is not necessarily beneficial to the company.
Human In The Loop (HITL)
HITL in procurement is a process in which internal and external procurement related data is fed into an AI
system. The system, makes prediction on the most suitable supplier for acquiring goods, services, or work.
However, in this case the AI system suggests sourcing decision insights procurement specialist of the firm.
The AI system only acts as an aiding system, which gives a short list of external sources, that are most cost-
efficient, based on the data analyzed by the system. The final decision is made by the procurement specialist
and not by the AI system. This can be advantageous because the data collected can have some degree of
uncertainty, that would make the predictions not 100% accurate. Therefore, the specialists can review the

Description of Methodology
A questionnaire was sent out to collect primary data from employees working in procurement or related
business departments in various oil and gas companies. The questionnaire has used a five-point Likert
agreement scale. The survey questions cover the following areas (Bienhaus, F. et al, 2018):
1.
Impact of digital transformation
2.
Potential challenges in digital transformation of procurement and supply chain
3.
Enabling technology to leverage procurement
The findings and observations of the study are used to measure the benefits of implementing cognitive
procurement in the oil and gas industry 4.0. The new transformed roles of procurement for industry 4.0 are
also discussed in the conclusion, which includes:
– Daily activities
– Procurement decision-making activities
– Strategic Sourcing
– Procurement to drive organizational profitability, effectiveness and efficiency
Survey Data and Results
Data for this study were obtained by compiling a list of relevant questions from multiple sources. An online
survey questionnaire was sent to procurement professionals in various organizations in the oil and gas
industry. The results were presented based on the percentage of respective responses from the participants
for each questionnaire conducted as part of the study.
The survey on the impact of digital transformation (Fig. 10) on procurement was conducted with 5
questions. 88.9% of the participants either agreed or strongly agreed that procurement will transform into
a strategic and innovative function. Procurement will be a function to support efficiency, effectiveness and
profitability. 92.6% of participants either agreed or strongly agreed that Integrating Market Intelligence data
from various external sources for procurement will give the organization better insight for procurement
decisions. Majority of the participants also agree or strongly agree that artificial intelligence will be able
to reduce day-to-day procurement activities and 70.9% of the participants believe that AI can take over the
procurement process in the long run. In summary, the survey result shows that digital transformation will
impact the procurement function of oil and gas companies.
